title: Dormant Account Reactivation Following Credential Stuffing Wave
narrative: Account inactive for 180+ days suddenly initiates transactions within 2 hours of a detected credential stuffing attack targeting the merchant. Attacker successfully authenticates using compromised credentials from a third-party breach. First post-dormancy transaction typically tests card validity with $4.99-$9.99 digital purchase (ProductCD=W), followed within 20-45 minutes by 3-5 higher-value transactions ($299-$899) to different shipping addresses. Email domain remains unchanged but delivery addr1 shows 800+ mile distance (dist1) from historical shipping locations. Device fingerprint completely new: different DeviceType, id_30 browser signature, and IP geolocation. card1_txn_count_7day spikes from 0 to 6-9. No failed login attempts visible because credentials were valid. AVS mismatch common on subsequent orders. Pattern peaks during non-business hours (txn_hour 22-04) when legitimate user unlikely to shop after long absence.
indicators: Account dormant 180+ days, then 4-8 transactions within 3-hour window
First transaction $4.99-$9.99 digital good, followed by $299-$899 physical goods within 45 minutes
dist1 shows 800+ miles between new addr1 and all historical shipping addresses
Complete device fingerprint change: new DeviceType, id_30, id_31, IP subnet
Transaction timing txn_hour 22-04 (night hours) with is_night=1
AVS mismatch on 60%+ of post-reactivation transactions despite valid CVV
summary: An account that hasn't been used in over 6 months suddenly makes 4-8 purchases within 3 hours, starting with a small digital item then quickly escalating to multiple high-value orders shipped to new addresses 800+ miles away, all from a completely different device and browser.
reasoning: A fraudster has obtained valid login credentials from a data breach and is racing to extract maximum value before the legitimate owner notices, testing the card first with a small charge then rapidly placing expensive orders to addresses they control.
indicator_explanations: Long-inactive account suddenly bursts back to life with rapid transaction cluster
Small test purchase validates card before rapid escalation to expensive items
Shipping address is nowhere near any location the account has used before
Every device identifier has changed indicating takeover from completely new environment
Card goes from zero activity to sustained burst within single week
Activity clusters during overnight hours when legitimate user likely asleep
Billing address fails verification repeatedly despite attacker having valid card details
